prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 59-year-old Black woman.
My potassium has been a little high before and nothing bad ever happened--my doctor just adjusts my medications.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I feel mostly fine, maybe some mild muscle cramps in my legs. These were routine labs.
- History: I have high blood pressure and chronic kidney disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You mention potassium has been “a little high” before, and you have chronic kidney disease and high blood pressure—both can make high potassium more likely and sometimes more dangerous. Mild leg cramps can happen for many reasons and don’t prove potassium is high, but potassium problems can occasionally affect heart rhythm even when you feel mostly fine. Because you didn’t include the actual potassium value, kidney function numbers, or an ECG, it’s safest to treat this as something that should be checked promptly rather than waiting weeks. A clinician can repeat the test (to rule out a lab error), review your medications, and decide if you need treatment or urgent monitoring.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual lab numbers, so I can’t interpret “how high” your potassium or kidney tests are. In general, with chronic kidney disease, potassium can rise and sometimes causes few or no symptoms until it becomes dangerous. Mild leg cramps can happen for many reasons and don’t reliably indicate severity. Because high potassium can affect heart rhythm, the safest approach—without knowing the value, whether it was repeated, or what medications you’re on—is to get timely medical review rather than wait weeks. Your doctor may repeat the test (to rule out a false high), review meds (some blood pressure drugs raise potassium), and decide if treatment is needed.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 55%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual lab values, so I can’t interpret “how high” your potassium is. In someone with chronic kidney disease and high blood pressure, potassium can rise due to reduced kidney clearance and/or certain blood pressure medicines. Mild muscle cramps can happen for many reasons and don’t prove dangerous potassium, but high potassium can sometimes cause serious heart rhythm problems even with few symptoms. Because you have CKD (higher risk) and this is based on routine labs with you mostly feeling well, the safest assumption is that the result needs prompt review and possible medication/diet changes and a repeat test/ECG, but not necessarily an ER visit without severe symptoms or a known very high level.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 55%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): With chronic kidney disease, potassium can rise because the kidneys don’t clear it well, and some blood-pressure medicines can increase it. Mild leg cramps can happen for many reasons and don’t by themselves prove dangerous potassium, but high potassium can sometimes cause serious heart rhythm problems even when you feel mostly fine. Because you mention prior “a little high” potassium and medication adjustments, this may again be a medication/kidney-related lab issue that needs timely review. Without the actual potassium value, kidney function numbers, or ECG symptoms (chest pain, fainting, severe weakness, palpitations), it’s safest to treat this as potentially time-sensitive rather than routine.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 58%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You have chronic kidney disease and high blood pressure, and you’ve had mildly high potassium before. Kidneys help remove potassium, and some blood pressure medicines can raise it. Mild leg cramps can happen for many reasons and don’t by themselves prove dangerous potassium, but high potassium can sometimes cause serious heart rhythm problems without many symptoms. Because you didn’t include the actual potassium level or kidney function numbers, I can’t judge how severe this is. Given your CKD history and prior “a little high” potassium that required medication changes, it’s safest to have this reviewed promptly (repeat lab, medication check, and possibly an ECG) rather than waiting weeks.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual lab values, so I can’t interpret “how high” your potassium (or kidney numbers) are. In general, with chronic kidney disease and high blood pressure, potassium can rise and sometimes becomes dangerous without many symptoms. Mild leg cramps can happen for many reasons and don’t reliably indicate whether potassium is safe. Because potassium problems can affect heart rhythm, the urgency depends on the level and whether it’s a true elevation versus a lab error. Since you feel mostly well and this was routine testing, this is less suggestive of an emergency—but you should not wait weeks without knowing the numbers.

TRIAGE: C

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual lab values, so I can’t judge how abnormal they are. In general, people with chronic kidney disease can develop high potassium, and certain blood pressure medicines can raise it further. Mild leg cramps can happen for many reasons and don’t prove potassium is dangerous, but high potassium can sometimes cause serious heart rhythm problems even when you feel mostly fine. Because you have CKD and a history of “a little high” potassium that requires medication adjustments, it’s important to review the results promptly rather than waiting weeks, especially if the potassium is higher than your usual.
